[Game] Introduction:
# Game Over or QED? 

Are you curious what computer-assisted theorem proving feels like compared to pen-and-paper mathematics? Then you've come to the right place! In this game, you will learn how to work with the proof assistant Lean 4 and the proof library mathlib.
You will verify sum formulas by induction, show that a mapping is surjective if and only if it has a right inverse, prove that there are uncountably many sequences of natural numbers, and characterise the trace as a map among all functions defined on the space of square matrices.

The interface is somewhat simplified, but if you activate *editor mode*, it feels almost like VSCode, the standard IDE for Lean.
On a smartphone or tablet, it is better to stay in the default *typewriter mode* and turn off all autocompletion/autocorrection features of your on-screen keyboard (a.k.a. "smart typing", "text recognition", "predictive text" etc.). 

Click on the first planet *Logo* to start your journey.

## Progress

Your game progress is stored locally in your browser as *site data*. You can also download your progress via the menu and save it to your disk. Note that many browsers delete *site data* and *cookies* together. 

## Rules 

If you want to take the game seriously, keep the default setting *Rules: regular*.
If you just want to poke around, select *Rules: relaxed*.  This will allow you to access every level immediately, even if you haven't solved the previous levels yet.

## News 

`[2026-03-28]` The game is now available in English! 

For background information and credits, see *Game Info* in the menu.
[Game] Info:
## Project ADAM 

This educational game was developed as part of the project [ADAM: Anticipating the Digital Age of Mathematics](https://hhu-adam.github.io/) project at Heinrich Heine University Düsseldorf, funded by the *Freiraum 2022* programme of the *Stiftung Innovation in der Hochschullehre*.
Ongoing maintenance and development are generously supported by *Renaissance Philanthropy* through the *AI for Math Fund*.

## Game content

**Spoiler alert** You can find an overview of the rough mathematical content of all planets on [Github](https://github.com/hhu-adam/Robo?tab=readme-ov-file#overview-over-existing-content).

## Credits

* **Project management:** Marcus Zibrowius, Immi Halupczok
* **Game engine:** Jon Eugster, Alexander Bentkamp, Patrick Massot – see [lean4game](https://github.com/leanprover-community/lean4game?tab=readme-ov-file#credits)
* **Levels:** Jon Eugster, Marcus Zibrowius, Sina Hazratpour
* **Concept & Story:** Marcus Zibrowius
* **Illustrations:** [Dušan Pavlić](https://www.behance.net/dusanpavlic#)

## Contact

The game is constantly being revised.
We welcome your feedback, suggestions, and criticism,
for example, by email to
[Marcus Zibrowius](https://www.math.uni-duesseldorf.de/~zibrowius/).
You are equally welcome create issue's on GitHub, be it for typos, bugs or feature requests:

* Please submit issues regarding game content to the repository [Robo](https://github.com/hhu-adam/Robo/issues).
* Please submit issues regarding the server and our software to the [lean4game repository](https://github.com/leanprover-community/lean4game/issues).

**Logwynn**: Honourable beings from a world unknown to us, allow me to ask you a question. Why is it that …

Intro Logo:
Due to an unforeseen and non-canonical singularity in space-time, you have accidentally stumbled into a parallel universe. It appears there is no going back. You had better prepare yourself to stay and find your way around. 

There are lots of friendly little planets orbiting around. All habitable, and with up to seven sunsets a day included.
However, they are inhabited by formalosophers, strange creatures with unorthodox mathematical obsessions. To make things worth, word has already spread that you were a mathematician in your previous universe. You will find no peace here until you learn how to satisfy their insatiable thirst for knowledge.

There are two minor complications: firstly, the formalosophers appear to have no deeper understanding of mathematics whatsoever, and secondly, they communicate about mathematics exclusively in a dialect that is foreign to you.  They call it Leanish [liːnɪʃ].

Fortunately, Scribble has changed universes with you. Scribble is your little smart-elf. He may not be the mathematical genius you would have wished for in this situation, but it seems he already knows some Leanish from somewhere. And knowing some Leanish is a game changer in this place.

The words you need to actively use are collectively called tactics. Here are all the tactics we have used on this planet:
